techniques.evidence_quote: the threat actors obtained AWS credentials from a sensitive file exposed on the internet... allowed the threat actors to gain access to the AWS account
the threat actor performed ListUsers, which returns a list of the existing users within the AWS account
the group performed the ListBuckets API call using the AWS Command Line Interface
generated both GetBucketLocation and GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration events
used WinSCP to graphically view all the S3 buckets... The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access
The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.
the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets
the threat actor deleted a handful of buckets, which resulted in the DeleteBucket API call
they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization... the victim organization had one week to pay
attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#

attack_chain.description: Attackers obtained AWS access keys belonging to an IAM user from a sensitive file exposed on the internet and used them to authenticate to the victim's AWS account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actors obtained AWS credentials from a sensitive file exposed on the internet... the group specifically targeted the exposed AWS access key belonging to an IAM user

attack_chain.description: Threat actor called IAM ListUsers to enumerate users in the AWS account; the call failed due to limited permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor performed ListUsers... Due to the limited permissions associated with the access key, the API call failed.

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the AWS CLI to run ListBuckets to enumerate S3 buckets in the account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the group performed the ListBuckets API call using the AWS Command Line Interface

attack_chain.description: Attacker switched to S3 Browser to iterate through S3 buckets, generating GetBucketLocation and GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration events.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actors switched to using the S3 Browser tool to iterate through the S3 buckets in the account and that generated both GetBucketLocation and GetBucketObjectLockConfiguration events

attack_chain.description: After nearly a month of dormancy, attackers returned and used WinSCP to access all S3 buckets and their contents.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor group used WinSCP to graphically view all the S3 buckets in the account

attack_chain.description: Attackers exfiltrated S3 data (proven later in the ransom note). No object-level logging was enabled, so exact exfiltration actions were not recorded.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.

evidence_chunks.quote: The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.
The ransom note later sent by the threat actor provided proof of data access, but it did not provide enough specifics to determine what S3 data left the environment.

attack_chain.description: Attacker deleted a selection of S3 buckets via DeleteBucket API calls.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor deleted a handful of buckets, which resulted in the DeleteBucket API call

attack_chain.description: Attacker used an automated script with the AWS CLI to create new S3 buckets with naming scheme 'contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#'.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#

evidence_chunks.quote: the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#
the threat actor used an automated script with the AWS CLI and attempted to create new S3 buckets. These buckets had various name variations of contact-shinycorp-tutanota-com-#

attack_chain.description: Threat actor sent an extortion email demanding payment within one week, threatening consequences for the victim's private data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization. The note stated the group had shifted to extortion to make more money and that the victim organization had one week to pay

evidence_chunks.quote: they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization. The note stated the group had shifted to extortion to make more money and that the victim organization had one week to pay

they completed their attack by sending an extortion email to the victim organization. The note stated the group had shifted to extortion to make more money and that the victim organization had one week to pay

initial_access_vector: AWS access keys for an IAM user were exposed in a sensitive file on a public internet repository; Bling Libra harvested and reused these valid credentials to access the AWS account.
persistence_mechanism: None observed; attackers continued to use the compromised long-lived access key (valid account).
privilege_escalation: None; attackers were constrained by the AmazonS3FullAccess permissions attached to the compromised IAM user and did not escalate.
